data.title: AZZ reiterates fiscal 2027 outlook for $1.725B-$1.775B sales and $6.50-$7.00 adjusted EPS, backed by data center and T&D demand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: AZZ Inc. (AZZ) Q4 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • “We delivered a strong close to the year and achieved record sales and profitability for the third consecutive year.” (President, CEO & Director Thomas Ferguson)
  • “Full year sales totaled $1.65 billion. Adjusted EBITDA surpassed $367 million and adjusted earnings per share grew 19% year-over-year to $6.19.” (President, CEO & Director Ferguson)
  • “A key milestone was the completion of our greenfield Precoat Metals facility in Washington, Missouri.” (President, CEO & Director Ferguson)
  • “We further expanded our Metal Coatings platform last year through the acquisition of a galvanizing facility in Canton, Ohio.” (President, CEO & Director Ferguson)
  • “In fiscal 2026, which ended February 28, 2026, we reported record sales of $1.65 billion, up 4.6% from the prior year.” (Chief Financial Officer Jason Crawford)
  • “We reported record fourth quarter sales of $385.1 million, representing a 9.4% increase from $351.9 million in the prior year period.” (Chief Financial Officer Crawford)
  • “Looking forward, industry research characterizes the AI data center build-out as more structural rather than cyclical and the U.S. data center electricity demand is expected to roughly double by the end of the decade.” (Chief Marketing, Communications & Investor Relations Officer David Nark)

Outlook

  • “So today, we are reiterating our fiscal 2027 guidance. Sales are expected to be in the range of $1.725 billion to $1.775 billion, adjusted EBITDA in the range of $360 million to $400 million and adjusted diluted earnings per share in the range of $6.50 to $7.” (President, CEO & Director Ferguson)
  • “We estimate debt reduction to range from $130 million to $170 million in fiscal 2027.” (President, CEO & Director Ferguson)
  • “Due to our strong balance sheet and desire to provide above-market growth, we will remain selectively aggressive in our approach to M&A opportunities.” (President, CEO & Director Ferguson)
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s FY2026 outlook update, management moved from “We have narrowed the forecast ranges for total sales, EBITDA and adjusted EPS” to providing a full-year FY2027 framework and stating “we are reiterating our fiscal 2027 guidance.” (President, CEO & Director Ferguson)

Financial Results

  • “For our core segments, we increased Metal Coatings sales 14.1% and generated strong EBITDA of over $235 million or 31% of sales.” (Chief Financial Officer Crawford)
  • “For Precoat Metals, despite a modest 2.3% sales decline driven by industry-wide softness in residential and other key markets, we generated solid EBITDA of $176 million or 19.8% of sales.” (Chief Financial Officer Crawford)
  • “Operating income from the year rose by 12% to $265 million.” (Chief Financial Officer Crawford)
  • “The company's fourth quarter gross profit was $87.6 million or 22.7% of sales.” (Chief Financial Officer Crawford)
  • “Operating income for the quarter was $57.1 million or 14.8% of sales.” (Chief Financial Officer Crawford)
  • “Also in the fourth quarter, we reported a net loss from the AVAIL joint venture equity and earnings of $21.7 million.” (Chief Financial Officer Crawford)
  • “We reduced debt by $385 million and ended the year with a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 1.4x.” (Chief Financial Officer Crawford)

Q&A

  • Ghansham Panjabi, Baird: asked what growth is embedded for FY2027 by segment and whether Precoat volumes worsen; Chief Financial Officer Crawford: “somewhere in the mid-single to upper single digits for [Metal Coatings]” and for Precoat “relatively flat year-on-year.”
  • Ghansham Panjabi, Baird: asked Precoat’s residential exposure; Chief Financial Officer Crawford: “around 75% of their end markets are driven by construction and then around about 1/3 of that has that residential exposure.”
  • Ghansham Panjabi, Baird: asked about zinc and broader commodity trends; President, CEO & Director Ferguson: “from a zinc perspective, there hasn't been much effect,” adding “we try to keep up with pricing,” and “we're using surcharges to offset” fuel/transportation.
  • Daniel Rizzo, Jefferies: asked what else is weighing on nonresidential; Chief Marketing, Communications & Investor Relations Officer Nark: “project costs overall... go slightly up,” and President, CEO & Director Ferguson added “availability of substrate” constraints tied to tariffs and domestic supply ramping.
  • Adam Thalhimer, Thompson Davis: asked how AZZ is handling data center demand and how it is classified; President, CEO & Director Ferguson: “there was hardly a one single plant manager or sales manager I talked to that isn't working on 1, 2 or 3 data center projects,” while Chief Marketing, Communications & Investor Relations Officer Nark said revenue can show up in “electrical and industrial” or be “a little more clouded.”
  • Adam Thalhimer, Thompson Davis: asked for M&A pipeline detail; President, CEO & Director Ferguson: “We've got 3 or 4 in fairly active discussions. We've got one underway in due diligence,” and “I don't know that I project any of the larger ones for this year.”
  • Nick Giles, B. Riley: asked about capacity-expansion CapEx and EBITDA impact; President, CEO & Director Ferguson: “we're looking at adding kettles,” and on the North Texas kettle, “hopefully has a couple of million impact in EBITDA.”
  • Nick Giles, B. Riley: asked buyback appetite vs. M&A; President, CEO & Director Ferguson: “we're committed to minimizing dilution with stock buybacks,” while “I like using the cash for [acquisitions]” and “our guidance does not include the M&A incremental.”
  • Gerard Sweeney, ROTH: asked Washington facility utilization and ramp; President, CEO & Director Ferguson: “I'd say it's at about 40% now,” and “it's got to produce around 45,000 to 50,000 tons this year.”
  • John Franzreb, Sidoti: asked Washington revenue contribution and remaining allocation; President, CEO & Director Ferguson: “it was about $11 million or so in revenue,” and “we would hope to, call it, by the end of the third quarter to be in a position to start filling the balance of that.”
  • Eric Boyes, Evercore: asked paint pass-through and inventory; Chief Financial Officer Crawford: “the paint is not itemized,” it is “passed one for one through to the end customer,” and inventory is “around about 3 or 4 weeks' worth.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly positive, centered on sustainability of Metal Coatings growth, Precoat end-market headwinds, input-cost inflation, and capital allocation, including questions on “what are you embedding for growth,” “what are you seeing” in commodities, and buyback “appetite.” (Baird; B. Riley)
  • Management’s tone was positive in prepared remarks and largely confident in Q&A, using statements such as “We are confident that our strong financial and market positions will enable us to capitalize on strategic growth opportunities” and “we will remain selectively aggressive in our approach to M&A opportunities.” (President, CEO & Director Ferguson)
  • Versus the prior quarter, management shifted from enthusiasm about “we expect to release fiscal 2027 guidance” to firmer positioning around guidance execution, stating “we are reiterating our fiscal 2027 guidance.” (President, CEO & Director Ferguson)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance focus moved from tightening FY2026 ranges (“We have narrowed the forecast ranges...”) to reiterating FY2027 targets, while still describing early-year conditions as “it is early in the year.” (President, CEO & Director Ferguson)
  • AVAIL expectations changed from Q3’s “we are forecasting equity and earnings... to be 0 for the fourth quarter” to Q4 reporting “a net loss... of $21.7 million” tied to sale-related items and an adjustment. (Chief Financial Officer Crawford)
  • Analysts maintained continuity on core themes (Metal Coatings demand, Precoat softness, Washington ramp, M&A), while Q4 added more detailed probing on Washington utilization, substrate availability, and kettle expansion economics. (Baird; ROTH; Jefferies; B. Riley)

Risks and Concerns

  • “Excluding data centers, we anticipate nonresidential construction will continue to remain subdued in fiscal year 2027.” (Chief Marketing, Communications & Investor Relations Officer Nark)
  • “We expect the softness in both nonresidential and residential construction may provide a headwind for our Precoat Metals segment in the current fiscal year.” (Chief Marketing, Communications & Investor Relations Officer Nark)
  • “There's hardly a day goes by anymore that we don't get some price increase from suppliers.” (President, CEO & Director Ferguson)
  • “Availability of substrate... with tariffs on imports, domestic supply ramping up” was cited as a constraint that can affect customer ordering patterns and predictability. (President, CEO & Director Ferguson)

Final Takeaway

Management framed fiscal 2026 as a record year with segment execution led by Metal Coatings and a ramping Washington, Missouri facility, while highlighting AVAIL-related volatility in GAAP items. For fiscal 2027, leadership reiterated targets of $1.725 billion to $1.775 billion in sales, $360 million to $400 million in adjusted EBITDA, and $6.50 to $7 in adjusted EPS, alongside planned debt reduction of $130 million to $170 million and a “selectively aggressive” approach to bolt-on M&A that management said is not included in guidance.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Why Trump didn't really 'legalize it'
data.description:
On Wednesday, cannabis investors reacted with glee after reading a scoop from Axios that the Trump administration was preparing to issue an order moving marijuana from a Schedule I to a Schedule III drug under the Controlled Substances Act. Multi-state operators surged on the news.

After the development became official Thursday morning with a final order issued by the Justice Department, MSOs gave up much of those gains. Why?

A likely reason was a lack of clarity between Wednesday's report and the details in the DOJ's order. A department news release states that a hearing will be held on June 29 to discuss a “broader rescheduling” to Schedule III. Wednesday's action is actually quite narrow, only impacting marijuana for medical uses.

And while Cheech and Chong's favorite vice may eventually become legalized federally, the truth is that if that happens, it could take many months if not years. 

What follows is a Seeking Alpha primer on what Schedule III reclassification does and doesn't do.

No Federal Legalization

Civil and criminal penalties remain for marijuana possession and distribution on the federal level. However, given cannabis is now a Schedule III drug, those punishments will likely become less harsh than when weed was a Schedule I substance.

Currently, marijuana is legal for medical use in 40 states and recreational use in 24. Dispensaries operate legally in accordance with state laws in those jurisdictions. For the most part, the feds have steered clear of enforcement against these businesses and customers, instead focusing on illegal marijuana dealers and those with large possession of it.

Cash Only

For the foreseeable future, customers in states with legalization on the books won't see much difference in their transactions. They will still only be able to pay via cash. No credit cards, as US banks could face sanctions by accepting payments for a product considered illegal by federal law. Legislation to amend this has been introduced many times in Congress but has never passed both houses.

Tax Breaks for MSOs

This is perhaps the most immediate benefit for MSOs. A provision of the tax code, Section 280E, prohibits companies engaged in the trafficking of Schedule I or II substances from deducting business expenses. The companies will no longer face this restriction, a move that could lead to a substantial improvement to their bottom lines.

Increased Investment in Cannabis Companies

Because marijuana was previously listed as a Schedule I drug, many institutional investors were wary of investing in MSOs, most of which trade on the OTC market. Retail investors were more comfortable, but their capital is minuscule compared to what institutional investors would bring.

That is poised to change. An industry observer told Seeking Alpha that MSOs are likely to see increased investment from institutional investors and hedge funds. 

More Medical Research into Marijuana

Research into cannabis' medical benefits is likely to surge due to rescheduling. The plant will be much easier for scientists to obtain now. Researchers needed a Schedule I research registration from the DEA. Currently, there is just one location to get cannabis for research: the University of Mississippi. The institution grows marijuana for research under a contract with the National Institute on Drug Abuse Drug Supply Program. Some researchers have previously criticized the quality of marijuana from the program as poor.

Medical Marijuana is a Winner  

Taking a closer look at the DOJ's final order, it is evident the rescheduling will only directly impact cannabis for medical purposes, as well as FDA-approved drugs containing marijuana. The order applies to cannabis compounds that "are included in an FDA-approved drug product or are subject to a state-issued license to manufacture, distribute, and/or dispense marijuana or products containing marijuana for medical purposes ("state medical marijuana license")."

This could be an indication that if and when legalization on the federal level does occur, medical marijuana use may come first. 

Final Observation

The DEA's June 29 hearing is the next catalyst on marijuana's road to Schedule III reclassification. Although that agency under the Biden administration and again last year stymied reform efforts, the DOJ's order, as well as its leadership by Trump's former personal attorney and current acting Attorney General Todd Blanche, could make the DEA more likely to follow the president's wishes.

Multi-state operators: Ascend Wellness (OTCQX:AAWH), Curaleaf Holdings (OTCPK:CURLF), Cresco Labs (OTCQX:CRLBF), Green Thumb Industries (OTCQX:GTBIF), Trulieve Cannabis (OTCQX:TCNNF), Ayr Wellness (OTCQX:AYRWF), Verano Holdings (VRNO), and Jushi Holdings (OTCQX:JUSHF).

Canadian cannabis LPs: Canopy Growth (CGC), Tilray Brands (TLRY), Cronos (CRON), Aurora Cannabis (ACB), SNDL (SNDL), and OrganiGram Holdings (OGI).

Cannabis ETFs: AdvisorShares Pure Cannabis ETF (NYSEARCA:YOLO), Amplify Alternative Harvest ETF (MJUS), and Amplify Seymour Cannabis ETF (NYSEARCA:CNBS).

Cannabis REITs: Innovative Industrial Properties (IIPR), New Lake Capital Partners (NLCP).

data.title: Selective reaffirms 2026 GAAP combined ratio of 96.5%-97.5% and after-tax net investment income of $465M amid more competitive casualty markets
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Selective Insurance Group (SIGI) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “We delivered a solid start to the year, demonstrating the strength and consistency of our operating model in an increasingly competitive market,” John J. Marchioni said, adding, “Our reserves remain stable across all insurance segments and lines of business, and our underlying profitability reinforces our confidence in achieving our full year guidance.”

  • Marchioni emphasized underwriting discipline over growth: “We continue to prioritize underwriting margins over top-line growth,” and said Selective’s “pricing posture on commercial casualty in both standard commercial and excess and surplus lines fully reflects our view on current loss trends.”

  • On growth/mix, Marchioni said “premiums declined 1% year-over-year with E&S up 1% and Standard Commercial Lines down 1%,” and added, “In Standard Personal Lines, premiums declined 6%, while our target mass affluent market business grew by 1%.” He also said, “Our relative exposure to contractors has declined within our new business mix.”

  • On AI investments, Marchioni said “a significant portion of our strategic technology investments in 2026 is focused on improving risk selection, pricing accuracy and productivity,” citing that an “AI claims ingestion tool has processed more than 0.5 million documents” and that contractual-risk-transfer automation returns “over 90% of results… within 2 minutes.”

  • “For the quarter, we reported fully diluted EPS of $1.58 and non-GAAP operating EPS of $1.69,” Patrick Brennan said, adding, “Our GAAP combined ratio was 98.3%, including 6.2 points of catastrophe losses.”

Outlook

  • “We are reaffirming the guidance we communicated in January,” Brennan said.

  • “For 2026, we expect to see a GAAP combined ratio between 96.5% and 97.5%, assuming 6 points of catastrophe losses,” Brennan said, adding, “we expect our full year underlying combined ratio to fall within our original 90.5% to 91.5% range.”

  • On investment income, Brennan said, “We continue to expect after-tax net investment income of $465 million,” and added, “Our guidance assumes an effective tax rate of approximately 21.5% and a fully diluted weighted average share count of approximately 60.5 million.”

  • Compared with last quarter’s framing, management again pointed to first-quarter seasonality: “the first quarter typically runs a higher combined due to normal seasonality,” Brennan said.

Financial results

  • Brennan said, “Importantly, we had no prior year casualty reserve development at the segment or line of business level,” and added, “Our underlying combined ratio was 92.1%.”

  • In Standard Commercial Lines, Brennan said “net premiums written declined 1%,” and added, “Retention was 82%, stable with recent periods, but down 3 points from a year ago due to pricing and underwriting actions to improve profitability.”

  • In E&S, Brennan said “premiums written grew 1%” and “The E&S combined ratio was a profitable 89.5%, 3 points better than a year ago.”

  • In Personal Lines, Brennan said “the combined ratio improved to 92.8% for the quarter from 98.0% first quarter 2025 and 100.6% for full year 2025,” while “net premiums written declined 6% year-over-year with target business up 1%.”

  • On capital and investments, Brennan said, “During the quarter, we repurchased $30 million of common stock,” and added, “After-tax net investment income was $113 million, up 18% from a year ago.”

Q&A

  • Michael Phillips, Oppenheimer: “A little surprised by the downturn in premium growth.” John J. Marchioni: “New business is the biggest driver of the drop in premium… predominantly driven by hit ratios.”

  • Michael Phillips, Oppenheimer: “any comments” on shifts in retention cohorts. Marchioni: “the bigger focus area should really be at those extremes… and you're seeing that shift in a positive direction.”

  • Michael Zaremski, BMO: “would you allow the top line to start declining if the market doesn't move your way?” Marchioni: “We've been through this before… that short-term pain that we felt on the top line positioned us to really outperform significantly over the following decade.”

  • Michael Zaremski, BMO: expense ratio and operating leverage. Patrick Brennan: “we're obviously going to be very mindful of our expense ratio.”

  • Rowland Mayor, RBC: capital return strategy with potentially lower growth. Brennan: “our overall capital management philosophy is unchanged,” and “we repurchased $30 million of common stock… at attractive levels.”

  • Meyer Shields, KBW: absence of workers’ comp favorable development in Q1. Marchioni: “the majority of our action on workers' comp… came in the fourth quarter… associated with our annual tail study.”

  • Jon Paul Newsome, Piper Sandler: geographic hotspots and contractors. Marchioni: “Frequency and severity trends in New Jersey… have remained elevated,” and “our focus tends to be on the artisan contractors.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to skeptical on growth and competitiveness, with questions like “A little surprised by the downturn in premium growth” (Michael Phillips, Oppenheimer) and “would you allow the top line to start declining” (Michael Zaremski, BMO).

  • Management tone was confident in discipline and guidance, with repeated conviction language such as “We are reaffirming the guidance we communicated in January” (Executive VP & CFO Brennan) and “we continue to prioritize underwriting margins over top-line growth” (CEO Marchioni).

  • Versus last quarter’s heavier reserve-focused discussion, the current call leaned more into competitive dynamics and deliberate top-line moderation, while keeping guidance language consistent: “We are reaffirming the guidance we communicated in January” (CFO Brennan).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance remained unchanged quarter to quarter: “We are reaffirming the guidance we communicated in January” (CFO Brennan), after last quarter’s “For 2026, we expect a GAAP combined ratio between 96.5% and 97.5%” (Executive VP & CFO Brennan, Q4 2025).

  • The current quarter highlighted softer net written premium trends tied to new business hit ratios, with “New business is the biggest driver of the drop in premium” (CEO Marchioni), while the prior quarter emphasized recent-year reserve strengthening and margin plans, including “we strengthened commercial auto reserves by approximately $190 million in 2025” (CEO Marchioni, Q4 2025).

  • Analysts in both quarters pressed on underwriting discipline and expense implications, while management in both quarters framed technology as an enabler; this quarter added operating detail such as “AI claims ingestion… processed more than 0.5 million documents” (CEO Marchioni).

Risks and concerns

  • Marchioni flagged industry casualty pressures: “social inflation continues to pressure recent accident years,” and warned that “this could imply further deterioration in run rate industry profitability.”

  • Management highlighted competitive pricing risk in liability: “market pricing… has not adjusted upward,” Marchioni said, and framed mitigation as discipline: “we do not believe pursuing inadequate casualty returns will create long-term value.”

  • In E&S property, Brennan cited competitive pressure: “Property pricing was slightly negative, reflecting heightened competition and strong margins.”

Final takeaway

Selective’s management framed Q1 as a disciplined start in a more competitive casualty market, emphasizing stable reserves, no prior-year casualty reserve development, and reaffirmed full-year guidance for a 96.5%-97.5% GAAP combined ratio (with 6 points of catastrophe losses) and $465 million of after-tax net investment income, while describing deliberate pullbacks in new business hit ratios, portfolio mix actions, and expanded AI-driven productivity tools intended to support underwriting margins over top-line growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Altisource forecasts 2026 segment revenue growth as Hubzu inventory rises to over 18,800 assets
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Altisource Portfolio Solutions S.A. (ASPS) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We are off to a strong start this year" (Chairman & CEO William Shepro), adding, "For the quarter, we grew service revenue and pretax GAAP earnings compared to the first quarter of 2025 from sales wins and lower debt-related interest and transaction costs."
  • "For the first quarter, we generated service revenue of $45.1 million, a 10% increase over the first quarter of 2025" (Chairman & CEO Shepro). He attributed the mix to "71% growth in service revenue in our Origination segment" alongside "a 5% revenue decline in our Servicer and Real Estate segment" driven by "a onetime 2025 pricing adjustment benefit in our foreclosure trustee business."
  • "The Servicer and Real Estate segment is positioned extremely well with Hubzu inventory at 17,200 homes as of the end of the first quarter" (Chairman & CEO Shepro), and he later updated that "Hubzu inventory is standing at roughly 18,800 assets as of earlier this week."
  • "The company generated first quarter pretax GAAP income of $400,000 compared to a $4.5 million loss in the first quarter of 2025" (Chairman & CEO Shepro), and said the change was "primarily attributable to lower interest expense and debt exchange transaction expenses incurred last year."
  • "Net cash provided by operating activities was $4.5 million" (Chairman & CEO Shepro), and he added, "We ended the quarter with $30.3 million in unrestricted cash."
  • "First quarter 2026 service revenue of $31.4 million decreased 5%" in Servicer and Real Estate, and "adjusted EBITDA of $10.8 million decreased by 10%" (Chairman & CEO Shepro), which he tied to the foreclosure trustee pricing adjustment and "lower volume in our renovation business."
  • "For the quarter, we won an estimated $12.4 million in annualized stabilized service revenue wins" (Chairman & CEO Shepro), noting, "Two of the larger first quarter wins were in our higher margin foreclosure trustee and title businesses."
  • "First quarter 2026 service revenue of $13.7 million was 71% higher" in Origination and "Adjusted EBITDA more than doubled to $1.2 million" (Chairman & CEO Shepro), who said the acceleration "reflects sales wins and a stronger market."
  • "First quarter 2026 corporate adjusted EBITDA loss was $7.6 million" (Chairman & CEO Shepro), adding, "we believe corporate costs should remain relatively stable as revenue grows."
  • "Yes, I think we guided earlier in the year to positive cash flow, operating cash flow for the year" (Chief Financial Officer Michelle Esterman). She added, "you do see fluctuations from quarter-to-quarter depending on revenue growth, et cetera. But yes, we do anticipate positive cash flow for the year."

Outlook

  • "We anticipate this momentum to continue as the year progresses" (Chairman & CEO Shepro), while emphasizing, "We are forecasting full year service revenue growth in our Servicer and Real Estate segment" and "we are anticipating strong full year service revenue growth in our Origination segment."
  • "Our forecast assumes that our revenue growth is partially offset by lower Onity and Rithm revenue" (Chairman & CEO Shepro), based on "our estimated timing for both the service transfer of Onity servicing to Rithm" and "the transition of the cooperative brokerage agreement REO assets from Altisource to Rithm."
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s framing of 2026 as a period to "mitigate the impact of anticipated legacy revenue losses" (Chairman & CEO Shepro, Q4 call), management in Q1 emphasized acceleration from wins and pipeline conversion, stating, "Toward the end of the first quarter, we began receiving referrals from this new business" (Chairman & CEO Shepro).

Financial Results

  • "Total company adjusted EBITDA declined by $800,000 due to revenue mix" (Chairman & CEO Shepro), citing "higher revenue in the lower-margin Origination segment" and "modestly higher corporate costs."
  • "Net cash provided by operating activities was $4.5 million, a $9.4 million improvement compared to the first quarter of 2025" (Chairman & CEO Shepro).

Q&A

  • Timothy D'Agostino, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: "just understanding the quarter-over-quarter move a little bit more from $19.3 million to $11.7 million" in the Servicer and Real Estate pipeline and "what we should expect from the pipeline going forward"; Chairman & CEO Shepro: "the difference in the pipeline reflects the $10 million or $11 million in sales wins" and "we'll be working very diligently to rebuild that pipeline."
  • D'Agostino, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: "should we expect" operating cash flow "to be positive" through the year; CFO Esterman: "Yes, I think we guided earlier in the year to positive cash flow, operating cash flow for the year" and "we do anticipate positive cash flow for the year."
  • D'Agostino, B. Riley Securities, Inc., Research Division: asked whether positive cash flow is more supported by Servicer and Real Estate or Origination; CFO Esterman: "they both have positive EBITDA" and "more of the cash flow does come from" Servicer and Real Estate, while "we expect that to become more balanced as we move through time."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was neutral-to-slightly positive, including "Congrats on the quarter" (Timothy D'Agostino) while pressing on pipeline sustainability and cash-flow durability.
  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, with emphasis on traction from wins and inventory growth, including "We are off to a strong start this year" (Chairman & CEO William Shepro) and "we'll be working very diligently to rebuild that pipeline" (Chairman & CEO Shepro).
  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s tone shifted from positioning around anticipated Rithm/Onity roll-offs to reporting early win-related referrals and larger Hubzu inventory, while the prior quarter contained no analyst questions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q4, management set explicit 2026 ranges: "service revenue of $165 million to $185 million and adjusted EBITDA of $15 million to $20 million" (Chairman & CEO William Shepro, Q4 call). In Q1, management discussed growth expectations by segment and reiterated offset pressure from "lower Onity and Rithm revenue" (Chairman & CEO Shepro) without restating total-company ranges.
  • Operational emphasis moved from outlining "Project 45" and the stated objective of "a run rate of $45 million in adjusted EBITDA by the fourth quarter of 2028" (Chairman & CEO Shepro, Q4 call) to Q1 execution milestones, including "Toward the end of the first quarter, we began receiving referrals" (Chairman & CEO Shepro).
  • Analyst focus increased in Q1, concentrating on pipeline conversion and operating cash flow, while Q4 included "no questions at this time" (Operator, Q4 call).

Risks and Concerns

  • Revenue concentration risk remained tied to counterparties and transitions: "our revenue growth is partially offset by lower Onity and Rithm revenue" (Chairman & CEO William Shepro), linked to "the service transfer of Onity servicing to Rithm" and CBA REO asset transition timing.
  • Mix and segment volatility were flagged through profitability commentary: "Total company adjusted EBITDA declined... due to revenue mix" (Chairman & CEO Shepro).
  • Macro-servicing demand indicators were presented as improving: "90-plus day mortgage delinquency rates increased from 1.45%... to 1.6%" and "Foreclosure starts... were 5% higher" while "foreclosure sales were 27% higher" (Chairman & CEO Shepro), though he noted activity remains "significantly below pre-pandemic levels."

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 2026 as an early-year acceleration driven by sales wins, with service revenue of $45.1 million and improved pretax GAAP profitability, while highlighting that Hubzu inventory expanded from 17,200 assets at quarter-end to over 18,800 assets in April. Leadership reiterated expectations for positive operating cash flow for the year, but kept investor attention on the timing risk of Onity and Rithm-related revenue transitions and the need to continue rebuilding the Servicer and Real Estate pipeline after converting wins into revenue.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Freeport outlines Grasberg ramp-up to ~60,000 tpd in H2 2026 and ~90,000 tpd by mid-2027 amid higher $1.95/lb net unit costs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Freeport-McMoRan (FCX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Our sales of copper, gold and unit costs were better than our forecast and the favorable metal price backdrop allowed us to generate growth in revenues, EBITDA and cash flow compared with last year's first quarter despite our Indonesia operations operating at reduced capacity.” (CEO, President & Director Kathleen Quirk)

  • “A notable highlight of the quarter was the memorandum of understanding reached in February with the Government of Indonesia to extend their operating rights for the life of the resource.” (CEO Quirk)

  • “We reached agreement with our insurance providers during the quarter for a $700 million insurance recovery, which was the maximum limit under the policy. We expect to collect the proceeds during the second quarter.” (CEO Quirk)

  • “We returned approximately $300 million to shareholders in the first quarter including common stock dividends and the purchase of 1.7 million shares of our common stock.” (CEO Quirk)

  • “With the current material handling constraints, we now expect to be limited to approximately 60,000 tonnes per day from production blocks 2 and 3 in the second half of 2026, increasing to the 90,000 tonnes per day range by mid-2027 as modifications, the ore loading infrastructure are completed over the next several months.” (CEO Quirk)

  • “Our current outlook for net unit costs is expected to average $1.95 per pound of copper for the year compared with the prior estimate of $1.75 per pound.” (Executive VP & CFO Maree Robertson)

Outlook

  • “Our 3-year outlook for sales volumes of copper, gold and molybdenum… incorporates the adjusted ramp-up schedule for Grasberg… which is the primary change from our prior estimates.” (CFO Robertson)

  • “As ramp-up progresses, our second half volumes are expected to be approximately 30% higher for copper and approximately 50% higher for gold compared with the first half, driving earnings and cash flow in the balance of the year.” (CFO Robertson)

  • “We have incorporated recent diesel prices in our updated forecast and have also incorporated updated assumptions for higher gold and molybdenum prices.” (CFO Robertson)

Financial Results

  • “For reference, a sharp rise in diesel prices in March equates to an approximate $500 million cost increase on an annualized basis.” (CFO Robertson)

  • “We do not have significant exposure to the spot market, and we are further insulated to the sulfuric acid market volatility through our natural hedge from our smelters.” (CFO Robertson)

  • “Capital expenditures are similar to our prior estimates and are expected to approximate $4.3 billion in 2026 and $4.5 billion in 2027.” (CFO Robertson)

  • “Annual EBITDA would range from approximately $14 billion per annum at $5 copper to $21 billion at $7 copper, with operating cash flows ranging from approximately $10 billion per year at $5 to $16 billion at $7 copper.” (CFO Robertson)

  • “You will note we're highly leveraged to copper prices with each $0.10 per pound change equating to approximately $400 million in annual EBITDA in the 2027, '28 period.” (CFO Robertson)

Q&A

  • Carlos de Alba, Morgan Stanley: “Maybe I wanted to explore… the level of confidence that you have on the new guidance for Grasberg… are there any specific points… where you think there might be a higher risk…” Title Quirk: “it is really a construction schedule -- a delivery schedule from our vendor… there could be delays in getting the materials. There could be construction delays”; Title Johnson: “We've got the first one installed last week… we're testing that starting this weekend.”

  • Alexander Hacking, Citi: “How was this issue missed… And then… why not add more drainage to the mine?” Title Quirk: “there was nothing that was detected of any significance… it doesn't take a lot… it's just a small amount of moisture”; Title Johnson: “what the wet muck generation comes from the daily rainfall… it's only a couple of percent difference in moisture content.”

  • Nicklaus Cash, Goldman Sachs: “How established are the supply chains… and… is there any risk for the $2.50 unit cost targets for North America in '27?” Title Quirk: “we may have to have it made… the combination of additives and heat… is going to get us to the 800 million pounds”; on costs, “that will cause us to relook at the $2.50.”

  • Orest Wowkodaw, Scotiabank: “the idle cost and recovery costs at Grasberg went up to $1.3 billion from $900 million previously… Is that incremental dollars going out?” Title Quirk: “it's not an increase in cost. It's really characterization… we're just following the accounting guidance.”

  • Daniel Major, UBS: “What is the CapEx associated with these modifications?” Title Quirk: “We've added something on the order of $60 million to $70 million in CapEx… It didn't show up as a big cost variance.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone: slightly negative and probing on the Indonesia revision, with questions framed as “a surprise” (Carlos de Alba, Morgan Stanley) and “How was this issue missed” (Alexander Hacking, Citi).

  • Management tone: slightly positive but defensive on execution timing, emphasizing engineered fixes and monitoring, including “We have confidence in the ability to meet the plan” (CEO Quirk) and “This is the big one” (CEO Quirk).

  • Versus last quarter: management shifted from “we're on track for a second quarter 2026 start-up” (CEO Quirk, Q4 call) to detailed discussion of a revised ramp and bottlenecks; analysts also shifted from timing granularity to root-cause questioning.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Grasberg expectations tightened: Q4 described being “on track for a second quarter 2026 start-up” (CEO Quirk, Q4 call), while Q1 revised the PB2/PB3 ramp to “approximately 60,000 tonnes per day” in H2 2026 and “the 90,000 tonnes per day range by mid-2027” (CEO Quirk).

  • Cost outlook moved higher: Q4 expected “unit net cash costs… $1.75 per pound for 2026” (CFO Robertson, Q4 call), while Q1 updated “$1.95 per pound” (CFO Robertson), citing lower Grasberg volume contribution and higher diesel assumptions.

  • Major new long-term item: Q1 highlighted the Indonesia MOU to extend operating rights “beyond 2041” (Chairman Richard Adkerson) versus Q4 describing an extension “in progress” (CEO Quirk, Q4 call).

Risks and Concerns

  • Grasberg ramp execution risk centered on equipment delivery and installation: “the risks are that there could be delays in getting the materials… construction delays” (CEO Quirk), with mitigation via phased installation and local fabrication: “Our fabrication is taking place in Indonesia” (President & COO Indonesia Mark Johnson).

  • Renewed input-cost volatility: “diesel fuel… has been volatile” (CFO Robertson) and “prices more than doubled on the spot market” for sulfuric acid (CFO Robertson), with mitigation via contracting and smelter-related “natural hedge” (CFO Robertson).

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a quarter with better-than-forecast sales and unit costs, shareholder returns of about $300 million, and a $700 million insurance recovery expected in Q2, while the stock-moving focus remained Indonesia: a wetter ore mix created a haulage-level chute bottleneck that reduced the near-term Grasberg PB2/PB3 ramp to ~60,000 tonnes per day in H2 2026 and ~90,000 tonnes per day by mid-2027. Leadership emphasized the issue as timing-driven with an engineered fix, while updating 2026 net unit cost expectations to $1.95 per pound and pointing to diesel-driven cost pressure as a key swing factor.

data.title: First Citizens outlines $6.5B-$6.8B 2026 net interest income while shifting to a united brand in Q4
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Citizens BancShares (FCNCA) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "We were pleased with our first quarter results. This morning, we reported adjusted earnings per share of $44.86, representing an adjusted ROE and ROA of 10.39% and 0.97%, respectively" (Chairman & CEO Frank Holding).

  • "Deposit growth accelerated this quarter, up by 5.7% sequentially, anchored by increased client activity in Tech & Healthcare and Global Fund Banking" and "we continue to optimize our capital stack, returning another $900 million to shareholders through share repurchases" (Chairman & CEO Holding).

  • "We are expanding our commercial solutions and optimizing our brand portfolio to better serve our clients and drive growth" and "we will transition to a united brand structure in the fourth quarter, featuring innovation banking and fund banking sub-brands under the First Citizens umbrella" (Chairman & CEO Holding).

  • "In the first quarter, we delivered adjusted earnings of $44.86 per share on net income of $560 million" and "net interest income declined by $101 million, with NIM compressing 11 basis points to 3.09%" (CFO Craig Nix).

  • "Our NDFI exposure stands at $38.8 billion" and "83% of this book consists of capital call lines" (CFO Nix).

Outlook

  • "We expect second quarter headline net interest income to be in the $1.6 billion to $1.67 billion range" and "for the full year, we are marginally tightening our range to $6.5 billion to $6.8 billion" (CFO Nix).

  • "For the full year, we are reiterating our loan guidance of $153 billion to $157 billion, inclusive of the $1 billion in the BMO acquisition" and "we anticipate second quarter deposits between $171 billion and $174 billion" (CFO Nix).

  • "For the full year, we reaffirm our range of $181 billion to $186 billion, including the $5.7 billion BMO infusion" and "moving forward, we expect to pay down at least $500 million to $1 billion per month" on the purchase money note (CFO Nix).

  • "We are raising our adjusted noninterest income guidance to $2.12 billion to $2.22 billion" and "as we continue to focus on bending the cost curve, we are reducing our full year range to $5.34 billion to $5.43 billion" (CFO Nix).

Financial results

  • "The sequential decline of $6.41 per share largely reflects the impact of lower interest rates on our net interest margin" and "adjusted noninterest expense was $38 million lower sequentially, outperforming our previous guidance" (CFO Nix).

  • "Period-end loans grew $762 million or 0.5% sequentially" and "Global Fund Banking, which was up $1 billion on record production of over $6 billion, surpassing the record set just last quarter" (CFO Nix).

  • "Period-end deposits grew by $9.3 billion or 5.7% sequentially" and "SVB commercial client funds rose $8.1 billion to nearly $78 billion" (CFO Nix).

  • "Provision was $103 million for the quarter" and "the net charge-off ratio came in 9 basis points lower than the linked quarter at 30 basis points with net charge-offs totaling $111 million" (CFO Nix).

  • "Our first quarter CET1 ratio stood at 10.83%" and "we adjusted our CET1 target range down by 50 basis points to 10% to 10.5%" (CFO Nix).

Q&A

  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: "on the new CET1 target" and buyback pace; CFO Nix: "we would expect to moderate to the lower end of that range for the next 2 quarters" and "we would expect that headline net interest income and ex accretion net interest income to have already troughed in the first quarter, but the NIM will trough in the third quarter."

  • Casey Haire, Autonomous: deposit outlook after SVB-driven growth and NPL uptick; Elliot Howard: "we do expect continued growth through the end of the year" but "we did have some very large inflows" that may move off balance sheet; Chief Credit Officer Andrew Giangrave: "The increase was driven specifically by 3 main credits" and "we would anticipate NPLs to come down."

  • Anthony Elian, JPMorgan: request for software exposure detail; CFO Nix: "software is $8.1 billion" and "about $14.4 billion of exposure"; Chief Credit Officer Giangrave: "We have seen improvement throughout the last several quarters on our criticized levels within the software portfolio" and "as we go through the second quarter, we will be doing additional focal reviews at a finer segmentation."

  • Bernard Von Gizycki, Deutsche Bank: deposit competition and brokered vs. direct pricing; President of Commercial Banking Business Marc Cadieux: "the competition is intense"; Elliot Howard: competitors "still out there with really kind of a 4% type rate"; Treasurer Tom Eklund: broker deposits "in the high 3s" versus rates "north of 4%" in other channels.

  • David Chiaverini, Jefferies: guarded middle-market tone and loan pricing; Elliot Howard: "we do have a little bit of hesitation just with kind of the uncertainty that's out there right now" and "we're reaching more of a stabilization on really those spreads."

  • Christopher Marinac, Brean: purchase money note trajectory and broker deposit capacity; CFO Nix: "pay down an additional $500 million to $1 billion per month"; Treasurer Eklund: broker deposits "were really sort of the first deposits" off "a very low base" and "we still expect the direct bank to continue to grow" but may be moderated if brokers remain cheaper.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, pressing on capital deployment, margin trajectory, deposit competition, and SVB/tech exposure, including "competition that remains intense in deposits" (Bernard Von Gizycki, Deutsche Bank) and a request for disclosure that "has been disclosed" less frequently since SVB (Anthony Elian, JPMorgan).

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more precise in Q&A, emphasizing strategy and control points, including "we are confident in our strategy" (Chairman & CEO Holding) and "we anticipate a slower pace for the remainder of the year" on repurchases (CFO Nix).

  • Versus the prior quarter, management added more specificity around timing and trough expectations, including "we would expect that headline net interest income and ex accretion net interest income to have already troughed in the first quarter" (CFO Nix), while the prior call framed the path with "we expect that net interest income and ex purchase accounting, net interest income will trough in the first quarter of '26" (CFO Nix).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The Q1 call introduced a new strategic announcement on branding and platform expansion, including "expanding capabilities in payments, international banking and digital assets" and "transition to a united brand structure in the fourth quarter" (Chairman & CEO Holding), which was not part of the Q4 prepared remarks.

  • Capital messaging shifted from Q4’s "we expect repurchases to remain near or at" $900 million in Q1 (CFO Nix, Q4 2025) to Q1’s "we anticipate a slower pace for the remainder of the year" and a CET1 target reset to "10% to 10.5%" (CFO Nix).

  • Guidance posture was described as largely steady, with Q1 stating "we have not made significant changes to our previous guidance" (CFO Nix) while still updating specific line items, including "marginally tightening" full-year net interest income to "$6.5 billion to $6.8 billion" and "raising" noninterest income to "$2.12 billion to $2.22 billion" (CFO Nix).

Risks and concerns

  • "The macroeconomic backdrop remains fluid" and "deposit competition remains intense across all channels" (CFO Nix).

  • "a portion of this growth stem from large short-term deposits" and "we have already observed some anticipated outflows in April" (CFO Nix).

  • "we expect losses to remain elevated in the medium term" in "the commercial general office portfolio and the SVB commercial books" and "we are watching one larger deal that could result in elevated losses in the second quarter" (CFO Nix).

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a quarter of accelerating deposits, continued loan momentum in Global Fund Banking, and active balance sheet actions, while reiterating most of its 2026 framework. The company paired a Q4 united-brand transition and capability expansion with tighter full-year net interest income guidance of $6.5 billion to $6.8 billion, higher noninterest income guidance of $2.12 billion to $2.22 billion, and a lower CET1 target range of 10% to 10.5% alongside expectations for a slower buyback pace as capital approaches that range.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oklo rated a new Buy, NuScale a Hold at HSBC
data.description:
Oklo (OKLO) up 6.1% to its highest in more than two months after saying it is working with Nvidia and Los Alamos National Laboratory to advance nuclear infrastructure and research, and as HSBC initiated coverage with a Buy rating and a $96 price target.

Oklo (OKLO) said the collaboration will focus on physics- and chemistry-based AI models, materials sciences and fabrication research and development, and power generation for nuclear-powered AI factories.

HSBC analyst Samantha Hoh said Oklo (OKLO) is positioned to leverage the new Department of Energy-led licensing process for its 75 MW Aurora powerhouses and fuel foundry, resulting in anaccelerated timeline for constructing and licensing new reactors, leveraging its expertise in ancillary opportunities of nuclear fuel recycling and radioisotope production.

With no debt and ~$2.5B in cash and equivalents, Oklo (OKLO) expects to book its first revenue later this year from the Idaho Radiochemistry Laboratory, which Hoh said is offsetting high upfront first-of-a-kind capital costs with customer prepayments and third-party investments, guiding to $400M of annual capex over the next two years.

NuScale Power (SMR) down 7% as Hoh initiated coverage with a Hold rating and $13 PT, saying the company's capital-light model of licensing its technology and providing pre- and post-construction services puts it at the mercy of customers in terms of the pace of execution.

While NuScale (SMR) books pre-construction service revenue from the Romania project this year, the company may be required to provide a second milestone contribution to ENTRA 1 as it advances a power purchase agreement with the Tennessee Valley Authority, the analyst said.

data.title: Nasdaq outlines $100M AI expense efficiencies by 2027 as it plans 23/5 trading launch on December 6, 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Nasdaq, Inc. (NDAQ) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Nasdaq entered 2026 with strong momentum, and our first quarter performance reflects one of the strongest starts to a year in our company's history." (CEO & Chairman Adena Friedman) "In the first quarter, we delivered $1.4 billion in net revenue" and "our overall annualized recurring revenue, or ARR, grew 12% year-over-year to $3.2 billion," alongside "10% revenue growth" in Capital Access Platforms and "18% revenue growth" in Financial Technology.
  • "In our U.S. listings franchise, we welcomed 15 new operating companies raising over $5 billion in proceeds during the quarter, including 7 of the top 10 IPOs." (CEO & Chairman Friedman) She added, "we see an encouraging environment for improving IPO activity entering the second quarter," while also citing "32% year-over-year growth of enterprise license agreements" in data.
  • "We were also pleased to announce that we will expand access to the Nasdaq-100 later in the spring with 2 new carefully selected partners, BlackRock and State Street," and "the pricing terms related to the index license for these upcoming new U.S.-listed ETFs will be consistent with the QQQ pricing terms." (CEO & Chairman Friedman)
  • "With SEC approval to extend our market operations to 23/5, we are focused on expanding access, resiliency and continuity for global market participants with the projected launch of December 6, 2026." (CEO & Chairman Friedman) She also said, "as stated in our initial announcement, we expect to provide early benefits of the Nasdaq Token design in the first half of 2027."
  • "We reported net revenue of $1.4 billion, up 13% with solutions revenue of $1.1 billion, up 14%." (Executive VP & CFO Sarah Youngwood) "This resulted in net income of $549 million and diluted EPS of $0.96, up 21%."

Outlook

  • "We are updating our non-GAAP expense guidance for the year to a range of $2.485 billion to $2.545 billion from $2.455 billion to $2.535 billion given the strong revenue performance we have experienced year-to-date." (CFO Youngwood) She added, "we expect a higher expense growth rate in the second quarter than the first quarter," and "we maintain our 2026 non-GAAP tax rate guidance of 22.5% to 24.5%."
  • "We are striving to achieve $100 million of expense efficiencies by the end of 2027" and "the majority of that will show up in 2027." (CEO & Chairman Friedman)
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management maintained the 23/5 timing specificity but now anchored the launch as "December 6, 2026" rather than the prior quarter’s "second half of '26, subject to regulatory approval." (CEO & Chairman Friedman)

Financial results

  • "Operating expense was $608 million, up 8%, leading to an operating margin of 57% and an EBITDA margin of 60%, both up 2 percentage points." (CFO Youngwood)
  • "In Capital Access Platforms, we delivered revenue of $565 million, up 10% with ARR growth of 7%." (CFO Youngwood) She said Index results were "driven by record average ETP AUM of $877 billion," while noting futures revenue pressure from "a mix shift in derivative volumes from higher-priced E-mini contracts to lower-priced micro E-mini contracts" and "a year-over-year decline in capture."
  • "Revenue was $517 million, up 18%, with ARR growth of 16%." (CFO Youngwood) She also disclosed one-time impacts in Capital Markets Technology: "2 one-time items which were termination fees related to M&A in Market Tech operators, representing 4 percentage points of capital markets tech revenue growth in the quarter."
  • "We had record net revenue of $317 million, up 10%." (CFO Youngwood) She added Market Services faced "lower capture in U.S. equities and U.S. options" because of "a mix shift towards lower revenue capture order flow."
  • "Nasdaq generated free cash flow of $629 million in the first quarter" and "we repurchased $548 million" of shares; "our Board has approved an increase in our dividend by $0.04 per share to $0.31 per share going forward." (CFO Youngwood)

Q&A

  • William Katz, TD Cowen: Asked about Agentic AI for internal efficiencies and client uptake; CEO Friedman responded, "we are striving to achieve $100 million of expense efficiencies by the end of 2027," and highlighted usage in "anti-fin crime," "surveillance," "AxiomSL," and "Boardvantage."
  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: Asked about Capital Markets Tech momentum; CEO Friedman pointed to "trade management services" demand and "a pricing increase," plus Calypso momentum in "collateral management" and market tech demand for "new asset classes."
  • Daniel Fannon, Jefferies: Asked about data strength and always-on; CEO Friedman said firms are preparing for 23/5 and that is "driving more demand for enterprise license deals" internationally.
  • Benjamin Budish, Barclays: Asked about sequential index revenue dynamics; CFO Youngwood cited "a mix shift in futures" toward micro contracts, and CEO Friedman added CME sharing tiers: "we've now gone to the higher tier as of the end of Q1" with impact "start to come in...in Q2."
  • Owen Lau, Clear Street: Asked tokenization timeline; CEO Friedman said DTCC has "an interest in trying to get to that first trade...before the end of the year" but "it's likely that this will still be an early...phase by the end of the year."
  • Brian Bedell, Deutsche Bank: Asked always-on impact on FinTech; CEO Friedman cited demand in "surveillance," "Eclipse" for 24/7, Calypso collateral, and more "colocation capabilities."
  • Y. Cho, JPMorgan: Asked incremental segments from BlackRock/State Street licensing; CEO Friedman said partners bring "unique investor universes" and reiterated, "the index pricing licensing terms are the same as for QQQ."
  • Elias Abboud, BofA: Asked AI model cybersecurity risks and adjacency; CEO Friedman said, "we're very careful in how we bring new models into Nasdaq" and "we will test models extensively" before use.
  • Patrick Moley, Piper Sandler: Asked whether tokenization/23/5 could transform IPOs; CEO Friedman said, "I'm not envisioning a fundamental change in the IPO process...only time will tell."
  • Jeffrey Schmitt, William Blair: Asked about outcome/event options; CEO Friedman said the first filing is "an option on predicting the future performance of the Nasdaq-100" within a "regulated market" framework.
  • Alex Kramm, UBS: Asked about one-time termination fees and future incidence; CFO Youngwood said future impact is "actually not very much" and "we're not seeing a sequence of those as a trend at all," while CEO Friedman added the ARR impact is "quite modest" versus the fees.
  • Michael Cyprys, Morgan Stanley: Asked 23/5 rollout steps and liquidity concerns; CEO Friedman said overnight currently has "about 2% of volume occurring today" and positioned 23/5 as "an evolution, not a revolution," with tape, operations expansion, and investor education ahead of December 6, 2026.

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive but probing on monetization durability and execution details, focusing on mix/capture, one-time items, and structural adoption questions (e.g., "what are the remaining hurdles" and "some hesitation...around potentially limited liquidity").
  • Management tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks ("we remain confident" (CEO & Chairman Friedman); "delivered exceptional results" (CFO Youngwood)) and generally explanatory in Q&A, including caution on tokenization timing ("it's going to be a little while") and IPO impact ("I'm not envisioning a fundamental change").
  • Versus the prior quarter’s broader strategic framing, the current call carried more execution specificity on timing and targets, including "December 6, 2026" for 23/5 and the "$100 million" AI efficiency target cadence.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The current quarter emphasized faster Financial Technology growth and highlighted cloud mix explicitly ("Cloud-based solutions accounted for 80% of ACV bookings") versus the prior quarter’s heavier emphasis on full-year milestones and medium-term framework updates. (CEO & Chairman Friedman)
  • The Nasdaq-100 licensing strategy expanded from prior-quarter discussion of index innovation to a new distribution change, adding "BlackRock and State Street" while keeping "the same as for QQQ" pricing terms. (CEO & Chairman Friedman)
  • Analyst focus shifted from prior-quarter market-structure topics (e.g., ATS flexibility, prediction markets) toward near-term operating drivers: Capital Markets Tech one-time items, index futures mix shift, and concrete 23/5 rollout steps.

Risks and concerns

  • Management flagged macro and market risks, including "persistent geopolitical tensions" and "concerns about the stability of the private credit market." (CEO & Chairman Friedman)
  • Index derivatives revenue headwinds were tied to "mix shift" toward lower-capture micro contracts and "a year-over-year decline in capture." (CFO Youngwood)
  • Execution dependencies for tokenization were framed around external coordination: "working with DTCC and with the industry" and acknowledging it will be "an early...phase" even if initial trades occur. (CEO & Chairman Friedman)

Final takeaway

Nasdaq management described Q1 as a high-momentum start to 2026, led by double-digit net revenue and ARR growth, record Financial Technology performance, and strong market volumes. The company paired that with specific forward markers—updated full-year expense guidance, a December 6, 2026 target date for 23/5 operations, and a $100 million AI-driven efficiency goal by 2027—while positioning Nasdaq-100 distribution to broaden via new ETF partners under unchanged QQQ-like pricing terms.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Citadel floats possibility of stopping NYC expansion - report
data.description:
Citadel suggested that it might not proceed with a major expansion in midtown Manhattan after New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani featured Citadel founder Ken Griffin's apartment in a video promoting his proposal to tax luxury second homes, according to a media report.

Griffin bought the Central Park South apartment for $238M in 2019, setting a record for the highest price ever for a home in the U.S. at the time.

"It is shameful that he used Ken’s name as the example of those who supposedly aren’t carrying their fair share of the burdens associated with New York City’s often costly and wasteful spending," Citadel Chief Operating Officer Gerald Beeson said in an email sent to Citadel and Citadel Securities staff, the Wall Street Journal reported.

He said the project to redevelop 350 Park Avenue will create 6,000 construction jobs and more than 15,000 permanent jobs in midtown. "The project — if we move forward — will entail more than $6 billion dollars of spending," Beeson wrote, according to the WSJ report.

Last week, New York Governor Kathy Hochul said she supported Mamdani's proposal to raise taxes on the wealthy with a proposal to tax luxury second homes in the city.

Mamdani campaigned for mayor, promising to raise taxes on the wealthy, but only the governor and state lawmakers can levy new taxes. 

In the memo, Beeson noted that over the past five years, Citadel "principals and team members (including nonresidents) have paid nearly $2.3 billion dollars in city and state taxes."

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Air Force seeks major increase in Lockheed JASSM missile purchases
data.description:
The U.S. Air Force is planning a sharp increase in purchases of Lockheed Martin Corp.’s long-range stealth cruise missiles over the next five years, according to newly released budget documents, following the weapon’s recent use in strikes on Iranian targets.

The proposed procurement calls for nearly 4,300 Joint Air-to-Surface Standoff Missiles, known as JASSMs, through fiscal 2031. Defense planners view the missile as an important asset for any future military confrontation involving China.

For the fiscal year beginning Oct. 1, the Air Force is seeking to buy 821 missiles, compared with 144 in the current year. Planned annual orders would climb to almost 900 in fiscal 2028, followed by about 860 missiles in each of the subsequent years.

If fully funded, the purchases would expand the U.S. inventory to more than 11,000 missiles by 2031, up from roughly 6,700 acquired before 2025.

The request comes as part of the Trump administration’s proposed $1.5 trillion defense budget, a 42% increase from the current year. The proposal is widely viewed as an opening bid, with Congress holding final authority over spending levels. Lawmakers from both parties have already signaled skepticism toward such a large increase in military outlays.

The figures released Wednesday don't include replacements for weapons used during the recent Iran conflict because the budget was completed before hostilities began. Those replenishment costs are expected to be included in a separate supplemental defense package.

The missile’s performance in the U.S. and Israeli campaign against Iran underscored its strategic value, with the Pentagon reportedly transferring some JASSM stocks from Pacific-based inventories last month.

The extended-range version of the weapon has a range of more than 600 miles and is designed to penetrate enemy air defenses while allowing aircraft to strike from safer stand-off distances. It can be carried by B-1B, B-52 and B-2 bombers, as well as F-16, F-15E and F-35A fighter aircraft.
